behaviour.mcq: Select one option. Answer revealed immediately on selection.

questions.stem: Which measure counts only NEW cases of disease?
questions.explanation: Incidence measures new cases; prevalence measures existing cases.

questions.stem: The denominator for an incidence measure includes only:
questions.explanation: Incidence uses the population at risk (excludes existing cases & immunes).

questions.stem: Person-time is the denominator used in:
questions.explanation: Incidence rate uses person-time (time intrinsic to the denominator).

questions.stem: In a case-control study of lung cancer and asbestos: among cases, 80 were exposed and 20 unexposed; among controls, 40 were exposed and 60 unexposed. What is the odds ratio?
questions.explanation: OR = cross-product ratio (ad/bc). Worked solution: OR = (80 × 60) / (20 × 40) = 4,800 / 800 = 6.0

questions.stem: The odds ratio is the characteristic measure of association derived from a:
questions.explanation: Case-control studies yield the odds ratio.

questions.stem: In a double-blind randomized controlled trial:

options.A: Only the patient knows the allocation
options.B: Only the researcher knows the allocation
options.C: Neither the researcher nor the patient knows the allocation

questions.explanation: Neither the researcher nor the patient knows the treatment allocation.

questions.stem: 'Equipoise', required for an ethical trial, refers to:

options.B: Genuine uncertainty about which treatment is superior

questions.explanation: Equipoise = genuine uncertainty about which treatment is better.

questions.stem: Which ethics document consists of ten principles formulated after WWII?
questions.explanation: The Nuremberg Code — 10 principles.

questions.stem: The Tuskegee Syphilis Study is chiefly remembered as an example of:

options.B: Research that was legal at the time but deeply unethical

questions.explanation: Tuskegee — legal at the time but unethical (no informed consent, treatment withheld).

questions.stem: In a cohort, 40 of 200 exposed individuals develop disease, and 20 of 200 unexposed develop disease. What is the risk difference?
questions.explanation: Risk difference = risk(exposed) − risk(unexposed). Worked solution: Risk (exposed) = 40/200 = 0.20 Risk (unexposed) = 20/200 = 0.10 RD = 0.20 − 0.10 = 0.10

questions.stem: When two factors together produce an effect GREATER than the sum of their individual effects, this is called:
questions.explanation: A supra-additive combined effect = synergy.

questions.stem: Standardization is used to remove the ___ effect of variables such as age so populations can be compared:
questions.explanation: Standardization removes the confounding effect (usually of age).

questions.stem: Direct standardization requires knowledge of:

options.B: The age-specific rates of the study population

questions.explanation: Direct standardization uses the study population's age-specific rates applied to a standard population.

questions.stem: The Standardized Mortality Ratio (SMR) is calculated as:
questions.explanation: SMR = observed ÷ expected deaths (×100).

questions.stem: In an occupational cohort, 60 deaths were observed. Applying national age-specific rates, 40 deaths were expected. What is the SMR?
questions.explanation: SMR = observed ÷ expected × 100. An SMR of 150 means 50% excess mortality. Worked solution: SMR = (60 / 40) × 100 = 150 → 50% higher mortality than the reference population

questions.stem: Meta-analysis is best defined as:

options.B: The quantitative (statistical) synthesis of results from individual studies
options.C: Analysis of a single large trial

questions.explanation: Meta-analysis = quantitative synthesis of individual study results.

questions.stem: Statistical power is the ability of a study to:

options.B: Detect an association if one truly exists

questions.explanation: Power = probability of detecting a true association.

questions.stem: Selecting controls by random-digit dialling of telephone subscribers identifies which type of controls?
questions.explanation: Random-digit dialling samples the general public → population controls.

questions.stem: During an outbreak, 500 people became infected and 25 of them died. What is the case-fatality rate?
questions.explanation: Case-fatality rate = deaths from the disease ÷ people with the disease. Worked solution: CFR = 25 / 500 = 0.05 = 5%

questions.stem: The 'healthy worker effect' describes the observation that:

options.A: Workers are exposed to more hazards
options.B: Employed populations have lower morbidity/mortality than the general population
options.C: Workers refuse to participate in studies

questions.explanation: Workers are healthier than the general public → biases comparisons using the general population.

questions.stem: Asbestos primarily damages health via:
questions.explanation: Inhaled fibres harm the respiratory tract (asbestosis, mesothelioma).

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a recognized type of environmental pollution?
questions.explanation: Technology is not a pollution type (water, air, soil, thermal, noise, light are).

questions.stem: Thermal inversion is:

options.B: A meteorological phenomenon that traps pollutants near the ground

questions.explanation: A warm air layer traps cooler air below → pollutants accumulate.

questions.stem: A clinic has 2,000 registered patients. On a given day, 300 have hypertension, 100 have diabetes, and 40 have both. What is the prevalence of hypertension?
questions.explanation: Prevalence of hypertension = people with hypertension ÷ total population. (Diabetes/overlap are distractors.) Worked solution: Prevalence = 300 / 2,000 = 0.15 = 15%

questions.stem: Compared with WHO air-quality guidelines, EU limit values are generally:
questions.explanation: WHO guidelines are purely health-based & stricter; EU limits are less strict (balance feasibility/economics).

questions.stem: The Mediterranean diet pyramid recommends:

options.B: Limited meat, roughly 200–400 g per week

questions.explanation: Med diet = limited meat (~200–400 g/week).

questions.stem: Which foodborne agent is notable for a high case-fatality (5–10%)?
questions.explanation: Botulism (C. botulinum toxin) has high case-fatality.

questions.stem: Bacterial dysentery (blood and mucus in the stool) is classically caused by:
questions.explanation: Shigella → bacillary dysentery.

questions.stem: How is climate change most likely to affect malaria?

options.B: Through temperature and rainfall changes favouring mosquito breeding

questions.explanation: Warming + altered rainfall favour mosquito breeding and shift malaria's range.

questions.stem: BMI classification cut-offs (e.g. 18.5–25 as normal):

options.B: Are not valid for all ages (children/elderly differ)

questions.explanation: BMI cut-offs are not valid at all ages.
